data.title: MPLX LP Remains A Top-Tier Pick
data.description:
Those who know me and who follow my work closely know that I am a big fan of the midstream/pipeline industry. I believe that this is an incredibly stable space that generates significant cash flows. Although many investors are attracted to this industry because of the yields that they can capture, I am more interested in how cheap these companies are compared to the quality that they bring to the table. One of my favorite players in the space has, for a while now, been MPLX LP (NYSE:MPLX). And it's a company that I have had a good track record with.

Back in January of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was based on continued growth that management had achieved, the attractive yield that the company paid out, its low leverage, and how cheap shares were. Since then, the stock has generated upside for investors of 2.1%. That's better than the 3.5% drop experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in September of 2023, shares are up 67%. That dwarfs the 28.7% rise enjoyed by the broader market. Even after all this increase, I maintain that this is a compelling opportunity. Because of this, have no problem keeping the business rated a ‘buy’ right now.

Shares still make sense

Fundamentally speaking, things are going really well for MPLX right now. Take results from the most recent quarter as an example. This would be the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue was $3.12 billion. That represents an increase of 9.8% compared to the 2.85 billion dollars reported one year earlier. This revenue expansion was driven largely by two separate categories of sales. One of these would be its service revenue, which jumped from $1.64 billion to $1.77 billion. And the other would be its product related revenue, which grew from $528 million to $687 million.

The service revenue for the company increased because of higher pipeline throughput that added $57 million to its top line, tariff and other fee escalations associated with crude oil and products logistics that pushed sales up by $24 million, and greater natural gas and NGL volumes and throughput fee rates that added $12 million to its top line. The rise in product revenue, meanwhile, was driven mostly by higher sales volumes in the southwest and Marcellus region that added $91 million in revenue, a $42 million benefit from higher NGL prices attributable to the southwest and Marcellus region, and a $27 million non-recurring benefit associated with a customer agreement that management did not provide much in the way of detail on.

To really understand the increase in sales, however, we should touch on each of its operating segments. During the quarter, the company’s Crude Oil and Products Logistics segment reported sales of $1.59 billion. That's up 4% compared to the $1.53 billion reported the previous year. The increased pipeline throughput and rate escalations that I mentioned already ended up being responsible for this. Total throughput ended up climbing 12% year over year from 5.29 million barrels per day to 5.9 million. Average tariff rates rose from $1.02 per barrel to $1.06 per barrel. The company also enjoyed a 5.6% increase in terminal throughput from 2.93 million barrels per day to 3.10 million barrels per day.

During the quarter, the company also saw growth associated with its Natural Gas and NGL Services segment. Overall revenue jumped 16.5% from $1.32 billion to $1.53 billion. This substantial increase was driven mostly by a 4.7% rise in gathering throughput that includes entities that have been consolidated into the firm, as well as any MPLX-operated equity method investments. Similarly, the amount of natural gas processed jumped 4.4%, while total C2 + NGLs fractionated rose by 4.4%. According to management, during this time, natural gas prices shot up 85.2% from $2.09 per MMBtu to $3.87 per MMBtu.

The rise in revenue has brought with it higher profitability as well. Although net income is not terribly significant in this space, it is worth mentioning that it rose from $995 million to $1.13 billion during this window of time. Operating cash flow fell from $1.29 billion to $1.25 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $1.36 billion to $1.48 billion. If we strip out preferred distributions, which I like to strip out because they are required cash payments, we would get a rise from $1.33 billion to $1.47 billion.

Whenever I write about companies in this industry, I also like to look at what I call ‘true free cash flow’. This is adjusted operating cash flow minus maintenance or sustaining capital expenditures. These are capital expenditures needed in order to keep operations running as they are. This ignores growth-oriented capital projects so as to not punish the company for its growth initiatives. Year over year, this metric grew from $1.32 billion to $1.44 billion. If we strip out the preferred distributions, the rise was from $1.29 billion to $1.43 billion. DCF, or distributable cash flow, increased from $1.36 billion to $1.49 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $1.64 billion to $1.76 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2023 and 2024 as well. This paints a picture of a company that continues to grow. In all likelihood, 2025 as a whole will be a positive time for the business. If we were to annualize results that we have seen so far for the year, we would end up with the table below. This shows continued expansion for 2025 and is a testament to the initiatives that the company continues to embark on. Of course, the end result here could vary based on acquisitions and the timing of said acquisitions.

On March 11th of this year, for instance, management announced that they had acquired the gathering businesses from Whiptail Midstream LLC in a deal valued at $237 million. These assets primarily consist of crude and natural gas gathering systems. In March of last year, the company used $625 million of the cash that it had on hand to purchase additional ownership interests in existing joint ventures and gathering assets associated with the Utica region. In February of this year, the company entered into an agreement to acquire the remaining 55% ownership interest that it did not own in BANGL, LLC in a deal valued at $715 million, plus additional earnout potential of up to $275 million. Those earnouts will be based on profitability targets between 2026 and 2029. On top of this, the company entered into an agreement to increase its stake in a joint venture that owns and operates the Matterhorn Express pipeline by 5%. That comes at a cost of $151 million and will boost the company's total ownership interest in that business to 10%.

Keeping in mind these uncertainties and additional changes that could occur as time goes on, if we use the estimates for profitability for this year and the historical results from last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are, quite frankly, low multiples for such a high-quality firm. On top of this, in the chart below, I compared MPLX to five similar companies using two of these different valuation metrics. What I found was that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, only one of the five competitors was cheaper than it is. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, two of the five ended up being cheaper.

There are other things besides valuation to take into consideration here. For starters, there is the yield that each of these businesses generates for investors. In the first chart below, you can see the current yield for each firm. With a hefty payout of 7.60%, MPLX is higher than any of the other five companies I compared it to. It's even above my favorite player in the space and a company that I own a significant amount of stock in, Energy Transfer (ET). In the subsequent chart, you can see the net leverage ratio of each of these businesses. This shows that, in addition to having a hefty yield, MPLX has far less net debt than these other companies do relative to the cash flows generated by the business. This means lower risk and greater financial flexibility and should, by definition, translate to a higher price. But as I demonstrated already, MPLX is one of the cheapest players in the space.

As time goes on, investors can expect the firm to grow even more. I already covered some of the investments the business has made leading up to this year. Over 2025 as a whole, management intends to allocate $1.7 billion toward growth initiatives. 85% of this will involve natural gas and NGLs. However, some of it will also be allocated toward the crude oil and products logistics category. Some of the projects that the company has in the works will take some time. As the image below illustrates, it has a number of initiatives coming into service in 2026 and beyond.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I am a big fan of MPLX. The company is a high quality operator in its space. It is continuing to grow both organically and by means of acquisitions. Shares are cheap compared to most other players. Leverage is super low and its yield is high. Add all of these things together, and I cannot be anything other than bullish about the business. Perhaps the only downside is that there is risk associated with its largest shareholder. This is none other than Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC), which has a 64% ownership interest in common units representing limited partner interests. More importantly, that shareholder also accounts for a large portion of the company's revenue and other income. Last year, in fact, this amounted to 49%. There is always a risk caused by such a high concentration of revenue and, in turn, profits. But for those who feel comfortable with this arrangement, I believe that a very solid ‘buy’ rating is appropriate.

Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and shares, when sold, may be worth more or less than original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted.

NAV return data includes investment management fees, custodial charges and administrative fees (such as Director and legal fees) and assumes the reinvestment of all distributions.

Effective February 28, 2020, the MSCI All Country World Index (Net Dividends) replaced the MSCI All Country World Index (Gross Dividends) as the Fund’s primary benchmark.

The Fund is subject to investment risk, including the possible loss of principal. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized.

In terms of individual stocks that positively contributed, BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY), a UK-based defence company, looks well positioned to benefit from increased defence spending by EU countries as the region seeks to reduce its reliance on the US. Shares of Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY)(OTCPK:ENGQF), a diversified European utility company, rose after the company guided to better-than-expected medium-term profitability targets. ING Groep (ING), a Dutch bank with operations across Europe, participated in a broad-based rise in bank shares as investors grew more optimistic about Europe’s macroeconomic outlook and its potential to support bank earnings. Not owning companies such as Amazon.com (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA) and Meta Platforms (META) also proved beneficial. The former two do not pay dividends, while the latter offers only a minimal yield, which is why they are not held in the portfolio.

In terms of individual stock detractors, shares of Target (TGT) sold off during the period amid declining consumer sentiment and fears that extremely high reciprocal tariffs on Chinese imports could hurt its business if not reduced to a more manageable level. Broadcom (AVGO) is a key supplier of graphics processing units for generative AI and sold off on investor concerns about future demand for AI chips. Blackstone (BX), a preeminent alternative asset manager, saw its stock come under pressure after the hoped-for improvement in the initial public offering market failed to materialise in early 2025, and as the risk of a full-blown trade war reduced expectations for the crystallisation of future performance fees.

Market review

Global equity markets ended lower over the period, weighed down by mounting concerns over the economic implications of US President Trump’s tariff policies. These fuelled fears of a global trade war, with early signs that tariff-driven inflationary pressures were eroding consumer confidence and could slow US economic growth. In April, markets sold off sharply after the announcement of sweeping new US tariffs – including a 10% blanket duty on all imports and higher rates on specific countries. However, equities recovered later in the month after the US announced a 90-day pause on most of the measures as part of broader trade negotiations, easing some of the immediate pressure on markets. Sharply increased duties on Chinese imports remained in place, though, intensifying tensions between the two countries. At the same time, persistent inflation raised concerns that the US Federal Reserve (Fed) could slow the pace of interest-rate cuts, despite signs of weakening growth, given its relatively hawkish stance. President Trump also sought to calm investors by affirming that the Fed’s independence was not at risk. Meanwhile, geopolitical tensions remained elevated, with US–Russia peace talks over the three-year Ukraine conflict failing to deliver a permanent ceasefire. Elsewhere, the German government’s announcement of sizeable fiscal stimulus measures for defence and infrastructure provided a rare source of positive policy news in the eurozone.

US equities ended notably lower. Despite falling bond yields and exposure to the fast-growing artificial intelligence sector, large technology companies significantly underperformed the broader index, as investors became more risk-averse and concerned about the competitive threat posed by Chinese start-up DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK). Annualised GDP unexpectedly contracted by 0.3% in the first quarter, according to an advance estimate, after growing by 2.4% in the fourth quarter. The decline primarily reflected a surge in imports ahead of the new tariffs, alongside softer consumer spending. The Fed’s preferred inflation measure – the annual core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index – fell from 3.0% in February to 2.6% in March, as expected, but remained above its 2% target. The Fed kept the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25–4.50% over the period, also in line with expectations.

European equities fell over the period. Eurozone GDP grew by a higher-than-expected 0.4% sequentially in the first quarter, according to a flash estimate, after rising by 0.2% in the last quarter of 2024. Meanwhile, a flash estimate showed that annual consumer price inflation remained at 2.2% in April, while the core rate rose from 2.4% in March to 2.7%, which was above expectations. The ECB cut the deposit facility rate by 25 bps at both its March and April meetings, taking it to 2.25%.

UK equities ended lower over the period. Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced £14 billion of spending cuts in the Spring Statement to help improve the UK’s fiscal position. The UK economy unexpectedly expanded by 0.1% sequentially in the fourth quarter after stalling in the prior three months, while annual growth increased from 1.2% to a higher-than-expected 1.5%. Annual consumer price inflation declined from 2.8% in February to a lower-than-expected 2.6% in March, though it remained above the Bank of England (BoE)’s 2% target. The BoE lowered its Bank Rate by 25 bps to 4.50% in February.

Outlook & strategy

Macroeconomic factors remain as unpredictable as ever. The trajectory of the US and global economies is dependent on the outcome of trade and tariff negotiations in response to President Trump’s “Liberation Day” reciprocal tariff announcements on 2 April. While a soft landing appeared to be the most likely outcome prior to the announcement, the risk of a recession in the US and/or globally is increasing, especially if countries choose to retaliate rather than negotiate. Nevertheless, our focus remains at the stock level, ensuring the portfolio is well diversified on both a regional and sectoral basis, and robust enough to preserve capital in periods of market weakness.

Footnote

[1] The MSCI AC World Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of stock markets of developed and emerging markets. For current holdings information, please visit abrdn Global Dynamic Dividend Fund - Portfolio Holdings

Important Information

Closed-end funds are traded on the secondary market through one of the stock exchanges. The Fund’s investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares may be worth more or less than the original cost. Shares of closed-end funds may trade above (a premium) or below (a discount) the net asset value (NAV) of the Fund. The net asset value (NAV) is the value of an entity’s assets less the value of its liabilities. The market price is the current price at which an asset can be bought or sold. There is no assurance that the Fund will achieve its investment objective. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

This commentary is for informational purposes only, and is not intended as an offer or recommendation with respect to the purchase or sale of any security, option, future or other derivatives in such securities.

Aberdeen reserves the right to make changes and corrections to its opinions expressed in this document at any time, without notice.

These statements are only predictions and actual events or results may differ materially. The reader must make his/her own assessment of the relevance, accuracy and adequacy of the information contained in this document, and make such independent investigations as he/she may consider necessary or appropriate for the purpose of such assessment.

The abrdn Focused U.S. Small Cap Active ETF acquired the assets and liabilities of the predecessor mutual fund, the abrdn Focused U.S. Small Cap Equity Fund, on February 18, 2025. As a result of the transaction, the Fund adopted the accounting and performance history of its predecessor mutual fund. Performance results including NAV and Market Price returns shown prior to February 18, 2025 reflect the performance of the Institutional Class shares of the predecessor mutual fund.

Performance information for periods prior to February 29, 2024 does not reflect the current investment strategy. Returns prior to December 3, 2021 reflect a predecessor fund’s performance. Please consult the Fund’s prospectus for more detail. Returns prior to May 22, 2013 reflect the performance of a prior, unaffiliated adviser.

At the stock level, Corcept Therapeutics’ (CORT) shares outperformed after the company’s key drug achieved positive study results for the treatment of a new condition, significantly increasing its addressable market. H&E Equipment Services’ (HEES) shares also gained after news of the company’s acquisition was released. Meanwhile, Stride’s (LRN) shares outperformed after the company highlighted strong execution and market share gains, leading to an earnings beat and an increase in full-year guidance.

Conversely, Vertex’s (VRTX) shares underperformed as internal growth investments for the company’s tax management solutions were expected to lead to margin compression and lower free cash flow generation. Steven Madden’s shares also underperformed as incremental tariffs were expected to weigh on earnings. Meanwhile, Workiva’s (WK) shares fell after reports indicated that the corporate sustainability reporting directive may be simplified, which could potentially affect demand for the company’s ESG reporting software.

Market review

US small caps ended the quarter notably down and generated a lower return than the broader US equities asset class, which also fell.

Against a backdrop of lingering inflationary pressures and a relatively buoyant labour market, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25–4.50% during the quarter. The central bank’s chair, Jerome Powell, reiterated the Fed’s commitment to sustainably lowering annual inflation – as measured by its preferred measure, the core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index – below the 2% target. President Trump recently put pressure on the central bank by calling for lower interest rates. However, the Fed aims to proceed cautiously. Investors are now factoring in the possibility that rates could be cut at a slightly faster pace than previously expected. This assumption remains in line with the Fed’s current projection of 50 basis points (bps) of rate cuts in 2025, down from its previous guidance of 100 bps.

Activity

We initiated Mueller Water Products (MWA), a leading supplier of fire hydrants, valves and other products used to deliver clean drinking water. We believe the company can benefit from secular growth drivers, such as the repair of aging water infrastructure, and that the approaching completion of a multi-year capital investment programme could potentially improve profitability. We also initiated REV Group, a manufacturer of fire engines, ambulances and other specialty vehicles. We believe operational improvements underway could strengthen the company’s leading position and support continued margin expansion.

Conversely, we sold Werner Enterprises (WERN) as the freight market continued to be challenged and visibility into a recovery was limited. We disposed of Cohu (COHU) as the automotive and industrial end markets remained challenged and, similarly, visibility into a recovery was low. Meanwhile, we sold Steven Madden to reduce the portfolio’s exposure to stocks that could be negatively affected by tariffs. We also sold Live Oak Bancshares over concerns about deteriorating credit quality among small-business-administration operators, who are the key demographic for Live Oak’s (LOB) loans. We disposed of The AZEK Company (AZEK) after the company announced its agreement to be acquired by James Hardie. Lastly, we sold H&E Equipment Services after peer United Rentals announced its agreement to acquire the company.

Outlook & strategy

After the quarter end, the Trump administration announced a new trade policy introducing 10% tariffs on all goods imported to the US, with additional tariffs that were calculated by country to capture the size of the trade deficit with the US. President Trump introduced the tariffs as a response to the rising current account deficit in the US and concurrent loss of manufacturing capabilities. This has led to significant market volatility as China, the European Union, and other countries had a range of responses to this policy shift. Concerns of a broader macroeconomic slowdown with higher inflation weighed on sentiment. As a result, President Trump announced a 90-day moratorium on all the additional tariffs, other than for China, while maintaining the 10% global tariff. During this time, negotiations with affected countries to strike new trade agreements are expected.

Uncertainty is particularly high in the current environment as the markets await conclusions from these negotiations. During such periods of uncertainty, our focus on investing in high quality companies should provide valuation support. Beyond this, portfolio holdings are being scrutinised for potential tariff impacts as well as sensitivity to macroeconomic headwinds. However, without greater clarity on where trade will settle out, we remain disciplined in maintaining a diversity of exposures within our high-quality holdings. Volatility is likely to remain high in the near term, so maintaining both defensive and economically sensitive positions is prudent as reactions to improved clarity could be significant.

We continue to believe that the long-term outlook for US small-cap stocks is favourable as valuations remain depressed relative to larger companies. We also believe that a combination of expected de-regulation and increased mergers and acquisitions should boost smaller companies. Finally, expectations for Fed rate cuts have risen in anticipation of economic slowdown, but the Fed has said that it will wait for greater clarity on the economy and inflation. While lower rates should ultimately benefit smaller companies, the persistence of higher rates in the meantime should benefit higher-quality companies over lower-quality peers. As a result, we believe that this combination of factors supports our positive stance on small-cap stocks in the long-term as well as maintaining discipline on higher quality companies to weather the storm from near-term volatility.

Important Information

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, fees, charges, and expenses of an ETF before investing. The summary and full prospectuses contain this and other information about the ETF and should be read carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus for the ETF, contact us at 1-844-383-7289 or download it from this site.

Investing in ETFs involves risk, including possible loss of principal. There is no assurance that the investment objective of any fund will be achieved. ETF shares are bought and sold at market price (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. As a result, an investor may pay more than net asset value when buying and receive less than net asset value when selling. In addition, brokerage commissions will reduce returns. Fund shares are not individually redeemable directly with the Fund but blocks of shares may be acquired from the Fund and tendered for redemption to the Fund by certain institutional investors in Creation Units.

The value of a security may decline for reasons directly related to the issuer, such as management performance, financial leverage and reduced demand for the issuer’s goods or services. The stock or other security of a company may not perform as well as expected, and may decrease in value, because of factors related to the company, to the industry in which the company is engaged, or to the market as a whole. The Fund is subject to the risk that the Adviser or Subadviser may make poor security selections. Funds that invest a greater proportion of their assets in the securities of a smaller number of issuers will be subject to greater volatility with respect to their investments than funds that invest in a larger number of securities.

Securities of smaller companies are usually less stable in price and less liquid than those of larger, more established companies. Therefore, they generally involve greater risk.

abrdn Inc. has been registered as an investment adviser under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 since August 23, 1995. In the United States, abrdn is the marketing name for the following affiliated, registered investment advisers: abrdn Inc., abrdn Investments Limited, and abrdn Asia Limited.
